Learn formal presentation phrases and polite interjections
Understand German professional praise culture
Review all grammar constructs from Unit 27
Office kitchen after the presentation. Colleagues stand around with coffee. Verumius at the center, still slightly dazed. Mia leans against the counter.
See? I told you so. Your presentation was so clearly structured that even Herr Braun laughed.
The boss said 'Well done.' Two words. But somehow they feel like a whole round of applause.
That IS applause. In Germany. Get used to it.
Later, Verumius checks the group chat. Lukas has sent a voice note: 'The Roman aqueduct? Hahaha, typical Verumius. By the way, the music school is running. First students!'
I would like to point that out
Polite presentation phrase.\nThe diplomatic way to draw attention to something.

A: I would like to point out that the numbers are outdated.
B: Thank you for the hint.
A: I would also like to point out that the date is changing.
B: Good that you point that out.

The polite way to draw attention to something in a presentation. More diplomatic than 'Achtung!' or 'Bitte beachten Sie.' Standard Vortrag rhetoric that signals respect while delivering potentially unwelcome news.
allow me
Extremely formal. From gestatten (to permit).\nUsed to introduce a point or politely interrupt.

A: Allow me a remark?
B: Of course, please.
A: Allow me to briefly summarize.
B: Please, go ahead.

From 'gestatten' (to permit). Extremely formal. Royal court formality surviving in boardrooms. 'Gestatten Sie mir eine Bemerkung' (Allow me a remark) belongs to the highest register of German professional language.
if I may
Semi-formal. Softer than 'Gestatten Sie mir.'\nWorks in formal and semi-formal settings.

A: If I may, I would like to add an example.
B: Please, go ahead.
A: If I may: I see that differently.
B: Of course you may.

Softer than 'Gestatten Sie mir.' Works in formal and semi-formal settings. 'Wenn ich darf, möchte ich ein Beispiel anfügen.' A polite bridge before making your point. Not as stiff as Gestatten but still respectful.
might I ask you
Konjunktiv II of dürfen + bitten.\nThe most polite request formula in German.

A: Might I ask you to send the documents?
B: Of course, I will send them immediately.
A: Might I ask you to speak a bit more quietly?
B: Of course, apologies.

Konjunktiv II of dürfen + bitten. The most polite request formula in German. 'Dürfte ich Sie bitten, die Unterlagen zu senden?' is more courteous than 'Können Sie bitte...' Subjunctive mood signals maximum politeness.
would you be so kind
Konjunktiv II of sein + freundlich.\nOld-fashioned elegance for formal requests.

A: Would you be so kind as to pass the microphone?
B: Of course, here you go.
A: Would you be so kind as to close the door?
B: My pleasure.
Konjunktiv II of sein + freundlich. Old-fashioned elegance still used in formal emails and presentations. 'Wären Sie so freundlich, das Mikrofon weiterzugeben?' is pure Höflichkeit at its finest.
it would be agreeable to me
Konjunktiv II expressing preference.\nUnderstated. The German way of saying 'I would prefer.'

A: It would suit me if we begin at 2 PM.
B: It would suit me too.
A: Would that be agreeable to you?
B: Yes, that would suit me very well.

Konjunktiv II expressing preference without imposition. 'Es wäre mir recht, wenn wir um 14 Uhr beginnen.' Understated. You do not demand. You express what would be recht (right/agreeable). Maximum politeness through minimal pressure.
may I briefly interject
Einhaken means 'to hook in.'\nThe polite way to interrupt during a discussion.

A: May I briefly interject?
B: Of course, please.
A: I would like to briefly interject: the number is wrong.
B: Thanks for interjecting. Good point.

Einhaken literally means 'to hook in.' You hook your point into the conversation. The polite way to interrupt during a discussion. Essential for German meetings where cutting in rudely is unprofessional.
I now come to the next point
Standard transition phrase in German presentations.\n'Nun' signals forward movement.

A: I now come to the next point.
B: Please, continue.
A: I now come to the next point: the finances.
B: Thanks for the clear structure.

The standard transition phrase in German presentations. 'Nun' (now) signals movement forward. Every structured Vortrag uses this between Tagesordnungspunkte. It tells the audience: one section ends, the next begins.
by (doing something)
Instrumental subordinating conjunction.\nDescribes HOW something is done.

A: He convinced the audience by giving examples.
B: By practicing, you get better.
A: You learn by making mistakes.
B: Exactly. By doing it, you understand it.

In + dem (the). Instrumental subordinate conjunction. The clause after 'indem' describes HOW something is done. 'Er überzeugte das Publikum, indem er Praxisbeispiele nutzte' (He convinced the audience by using practical examples).
by the fact that
Two-word connector. Dadurch (through that) + dass.\nMore explicit than indem.

A: By the fact that he was well prepared, he convinced.
B: By the fact that everyone contributed, the result was good.
A: Success arises by working together.
B: By the fact that we practice, we get better.

Dadurch (through that) + dass. More explicit than 'indem.' Emphasizes the means or cause. Two-word connector introducing a subordinate clause. 'Dadurch, dass er gut vorbereitet war, überzeugte er' puts the cause front and center.
by means of
Genitive preposition. Mittel (means) + -s.\nFormal and precise. Used in technical writing.

A: By means of a diagram, he explained the data.
B: By means of this method, we can solve the problem.
A: The analysis was carried out by means of statistical methods.
B: By means of practical examples, the topic became tangible.

Mittel (means) + -s (genitive). Genitive preposition. Formal and precise. 'Mittels eines Diagramms' (by means of a diagram). Used in technical and legal writing. The compact alternative to 'mit Hilfe von.'
Present arguments, introduce proposals, and weigh options. Academic vocabulary for structured debate and professional communication.
Learn verbs for structured argumentation
Present and compare viewpoints
Use transitional phrases in debate
with the help of
Takes Dativ via 'von.'\nSlightly less formal than mittels.

A: With the help of practical examples, he explained the topic.
B: With the help of diagrams, data become vivid.
A: With the help of colleagues, we managed it.
B: With the help of technology, everything goes faster.

Mit (with) + Hilfe (help) + von (of). Takes Dativ via 'von.' Slightly less formal than 'mittels.' 'Mithilfe von Praxisbeispielen' (with the help of practical examples) is everyday professional language.
chairperson
Adjectival noun. Vor (before) + Sitzende (one who sits).\nDeclines like an adjective: der/die Vorsitzende.

A: The chairperson opens the meeting.
B: The chairwoman has the floor.
A: Who is the new chairperson?
B: Frau Meyer was elected chairwoman.

Vor (before) + Sitzende (one who sits). The one who sits at the front. Declines like an adjective: der Vorsitzende (m), die Vorsitzende (f), des Vorsitzenden (gen). Gender-flexible by grammar. Every Verein, Ausschuss, and Partei has one.
to carry out, to elaborate
Separable: aus + führen.\nDouble meaning: execute or explain in detail.

A: We carry out the plan as discussed.
B: As I have already elaborated, the data are clear.
A: Can you elaborate on that?
B: Gladly. I will elaborate the point in detail.
Aus (out) + führen (to lead). Separable. Double meaning: execute ('den Plan ausführen') or explain in detail ('Wie ich bereits ausgeführt habe'). Leading something out from the abstract into reality.
to submit, to present a proposal
Not separable. Unter (under) + breiten (to spread).\n'Ich unterbreite Ihnen einen Vorschlag.'

A: I submit a proposal to you.
B: Please, submit your proposal.
A: We submitted an offer to the board.
B: The submitted proposal will be examined.

Unter (under) + breiten (to spread). Not separable. Spreading your idea under someone for their consideration. Very formal. 'Ich unterbreite Ihnen einen Vorschlag' is boardroom language at its most elegant.
to develop, to elaborate in detail
Separable: aus + arbeiten.\nWorking something from raw idea to finished product.

A: We still need to develop the plan.
B: I will work out the details.
A: The concept is well developed.
B: By Friday, everything will be worked out.
Aus (out) + arbeiten (to work). Separable. Working something out from raw idea to finished product. 'Ausgearbeitet' = fully developed. A rough idea becomes a concrete plan through Ausarbeitung.
to introduce, to implement
Separable: ein + führen.\n'Wir führen ein neues System ein.'

A: We are introducing a new system next week.
B: When will it be introduced?
A: The introduction is planned for Monday.
B: Good. Please inform all employees.

Ein (in) + führen (to lead). Separable. Leading something new into existence. 'Einführung' = introduction (of a system, product, or concept). Not to be confused with 'vorstellen' (to introduce a person).
to compile, to gather
Separable: zusammen + tragen.\nStrong verb: trug zusammen, zusammengetragen.

A: We compile the results.
B: I have compiled all the data.
A: Please compile the information by tomorrow.
B: Everything compiled and sorted.
Zusammen (together) + tragen (to carry). Separable. Carrying pieces together into one place. Strong verb: zusammentragen/trug zusammen/zusammengetragen. The gathering phase before analysis.
to refer to
Not separable. Ver- + weisen (to point).\n'Ich verweise auf die Studie.'

A: I refer to the 2025 study.
B: The reference is justified.
A: He referred to earlier results.
B: May I refer to page 3?
Ver- + weisen (to point/show). Not separable. Pointing someone toward a source. 'Verweis' (reference) is the noun. Essential in academic and professional presentations. Every claim needs a Verweis.
to hint at, to suggest
Separable: an + deuten.\nSubtle and diplomatic. 'Andeutung' is the noun.

A: He hinted at changes.
B: What did he hint at?
A: The hints were vague.
B: Sometimes you have to read between the lines.

An (at) + deuten (to interpret/point). Separable. Not stating directly but pointing toward. Subtle and diplomatic. 'Andeutung' (hint) is the noun. In German business, sometimes people deuten an rather than state directly.
to transition, to segue
Separable: über + leiten.\nLeading the audience from one topic to the next.

A: I transition to the next topic.
B: The transition was very elegant.
A: How does one best transition?
B: With a connecting sentence.

Über (over) + leiten (to lead). Separable. Leading the audience over from one topic to the next. The art of smooth Überleitungen separates good speakers from great ones. A clumsy Überleitung loses the audience.
to present, to introduce
Separable: vor + stellen.\nAlso reflexive: sich vorstellen (to introduce oneself).

A: I present the results.
B: May I introduce my colleague?
A: Please introduce yourself briefly.
B: I would like to present our new concept to you.
Vor (before) + stellen (to place). Separable. Placing something before the audience. Also reflexive: 'Ich stelle mich vor' (I introduce myself). The most versatile presentation verb in German.
to weigh against each other
Multi-word verb. Gegeneinander (against each other) + abwägen.\nThe core of German deliberative culture.

A: We must weigh the options against each other.
B: Yes, weigh all advantages and disadvantages.
A: First weigh, then decide.
B: That is how one makes well-founded decisions.

Gegeneinander (against each other) + abwägen (to weigh). Putting arguments on a scale. The core of German deliberative culture. No decision is made without careful Abwägung. Pros on one side, cons on the other, then decide.

In many cultures, praise is expressed lavishly. In German professional culture, 'Gut gemacht' from a manager is already high praise.

'Nicht schlecht' (not bad) is a genuine compliment.
'Passt' (fine/okay) means: everything is in order.
'Gut gemacht' means: you have exceeded expectations.

Germans tend to understate positive feedback and express criticism directly. If nothing negative is said, things are going well. When 'Gut gemacht' is spoken, you have done something remarkable.

In German professional culture, excessive praise loses its value. 'Großartig!' after every little thing wears thin. That is why Germans give praise sparingly, so it retains weight. When a German manager says 'Das haben Sie gut gemacht,' they MEAN it. The praise is genuine because it is rare. Verumius has just experienced this: two words that feel like a full round of applause.
This formula uses an imperative of the verb meaning 'to permit' addressed formally. It is the most elevated way to ask for the floor in German.
The subordinating connector that describes the METHOD of an action. It answers the question 'how?' rather than 'why?' or 'when?'.
He convinced the audience by using practical examples.
'Vorausgesetzt dass' introduces the condition. 'So... dass' expresses the result. 'Um... zu' formulates the purpose.
Later, at the window. The city glows. Somewhere out there the next presentation waits. But this time he knows: he can do it. In German. In his own way.
